transcript.transcript: I'm going to push record. The recording is going all right. Good afternoon Carol. I'm so excited to. Have a chance to talk with you about your life and the impact that you have had here in Idaho. Um, my name is HannaLore Hein, and I am the state historian, and I am also a member and past president of the Junior League of Boise. Today is Monday, March 30th, 2026, and I am at the home of doctor Carol McGregor to conduct an oral history interview with her about her experiences in the Junior League of Boise and her philanthropic work more broadly. This oral history interview was made possible with the support of the Idaho State Historical Society under the America 250 in Idaho commemoration. This oral history initiative supports the commemorations mission to advance the ideals of the American Revolution through meaningful community engagement and to recognize, appreciate and commemorate Idaho's journey in America's legacy. So, Carol, again, let me say thank you so much for your time today. I am hopeful that this interview will allow you to reflect on and share your memories and experiences with the Junior League and your philanthropic work across the Treasure Valley. Through these questions, I hope to gain insights into what motivated your interest in community service and philanthropy, and how opportunities for women have changed over the course of your life. I will begin with some general questions and we'll move into more specific topics from there. And I may ask some unscripted follow up questions just for clarification. But before we begin, can I have you state on the record that you are aware we are recording this interview? Thank you for inviting me to record an interview about my life and times. Excellent. I'm delighted to have you. Hanna Laura. Thank you. Awesome. Okay, so, Carol, let's just start at the beginning. When and where were you born? Well, I was born when my parents owned 80 acres out at what is now Barber. It was a town of Barber before I was born. Uh, and it actually is connected with my family history. My grandfather, McGregor, came here with the Weyerhaeuser Company from Minnesota in 1917. He was a logger for them, and they. They brought him to Idaho because of our great forests and the old northwest, which was Minnesota, where he was, where he started his that business. Uh, was being logged out. So he, he wasn't part of, the logging up the Boise River basin. But he was the Payette River basin, which is where my ranch is now. But so I do. I have actually written a little story about the the history of Barber and the lumber business, but they owned 80 acres out there and I was their first child. I actually sat on a horse before I could walk. We had horses out there and the old house is still there. So I was born June 12th, 1942, before Pearl Harbor. And how many siblings do you have? I have two younger brothers. Okay. And can you tell me what it was like growing up in Boise during the 1940s and early 1950s? Well, we moved to a logging camp when I was two and a half. My little brother, my first brother, was born in January of 1945 and that spring, my mother and the baby and I moved to the logging camp with my father. It was out of Council Idaho, in Adams County, up in rattlesnake country, uh, at a, at a place called the crooked River. And, uh. I wrote a short story about that, uh, part of my life. I, I think it's interesting because there are no more logging towns and it was a logging camp of people who worked there. And I, I can give you the story, but, uh, I won't prolong the memory at this point. It was a time that was very positive because we were producing lumber for needed ships and homes. Men were coming home from the war by then, and there was a huge, uh, housing market boom. And there was a big call for lumber. And those men that worked in that woods formed an idea in my mind about men that could do just about anything. It seemed like they were bigger than life and the trees they felled. I used to go out in the pickup with my father when I was very young, and, uh. And he would set me and his dog up at a stump and tell me not to move. And the dog was supposed to guard me, and I would hear them call timber and I would hear it fall. And I love the smell of that fresh cut ponderosa pine. And my father's cigar still takes me back to those times. Wow. Thank you. Um. Did you. We moved, fortunately, to Boise by the time I was six, because my grandmother McGregor died, and my father bought her house at, uh, Rose Hill in Hawaii streets from her from his siblings. So I was fortunate enough to go to school in Boise instead of council. And what was that schooling like? What do you remember from. The first grade was at Whitney. And I remember my teacher very well, Miss Campbell. She. She thought I was pretty smart. And, uh, and she was very, very nice to me. And I was talking way too much, I'm sure. Then then, uh, they built Jefferson school. And, um, I remember the little tiny sycamores that were in the front of, of the building were guarded by, uh, wooden structures so the kids wouldn't push them over. And now you can't put your arms around those sycamores. They're huge. Uh, so I went through grade school at, uh, second through sixth grade at Jefferson School and then, uh, South Junior High for three years and then Boise High for one year, and then Borah High I for two years. I graduated from Borah High in the second class of its history in 1960. And throughout that time, was your dad still engaged in the lumbering industry or did he do other things? He was a great entrepreneur. And, uh, and he was a very good father to me. Um, he, he was, he formed a company when I was eight years old called McGregor Triangle Company. He bought out Triangle Construction Company and they built roads all over the northwest. He built a lot of pioneer roads, uh, railroad relocations, highways, bridges. He bought Tony Marazzo company and Hanson and PA Bridge Company, and he constructed high rise. He bought a pole company. Um, and they set power lines. Electrical company was one of the subsidiaries. They set power lines across the snake River via helicopters. I remember when I was still in high school. So he did a lot of exciting things. His his real love was animals and ranching. But you can't buy a ranch unless you make money somehow some other way or inherit it. So he made money in other ways, and he began buying a lot of ranch land. And, uh, we had three sizable ranches. One was we called Black Canyon Ranch over on the Black Canyon Reservoir in Gem County near Emmett. Uh, our middle ranch was in the Brown Lee country in between the the drainages of the well near the drainage of the of Squaw Creek, which comes down from Ola and Sweet. It was up on the Brownlee summit from there and it was 11,000. Acres. It had beautiful spring. Um. Hey, uh. Dry land. Hey. One cutting and, uh, great range land for for cattle and a mother lode of rattlesnakes. And then the favorite ranch of all of us was the Barton Ranch. And that had been the land that my grandfather had logged. And my father bought this ranch when they were through logging from the Boise Payette Lumber Company. Mr. moon was the president then. So, um, we lived there in the summers from the time I was ten. And, uh, I always had a horse since I was six, but I would cry every, every fall when we had to move back to Boise. I would put my arms around my horse's neck and cry because it was so much fun riding all the time on the ranch. And there was another family up there, uh, who, whose father worked there. And we became very close as children with them and are still good friends with them. So it was a great, great life being a tomboy. Most of my life I was a tomboy. I loved to ride and ski and swim. Girls in my day did not have competitive sports. They had no girls teams at all. So the sports that I did were individual sports. I never learned how to play golf or tennis because I was living on a ranch. I was riding horses almost every day. We rode bareback a lot, which really, really makes you a better rider. But I was never agile enough to get on by myself. I had to get the horse next to a fence. The boys were able to hoist themselves up somehow. But I love the out of doors and I still do. What did that upbringing instill in you in terms of core values? I'm very independent, and I was trusted by my parents at a very early age to take care of my brothers and to perform tasks, and being very eager to please both of my parents as many older, oldest children are. Uh. I lived for their praise. I did grow up pretty soon. I was pretty mature for my age, pretty young, and I had a lot of responsibility. And I think that that, uh, when people talk about the eldest child, I'm quintessential, uh, in those sort of generalizations that people make about that. Um, but I mean, when I look now at how the world has evolved with constant communications and helicopter parenting, it couldn't be more different than how I grew up. My parents had no idea where I was. I wrote, I started training horses when I was 14 and and they never knew where I was. I would show up for dinner or I was there for breakfast, but I was rarely there for lunch. And I rode by myself all over the all over West Mountain and rode with the other kids. And when we had work to do in the corrals, I was worked like a man, and I've never. I never thought of myself as masculine. I love all the feminine things. I love perfume and fur coats and nail polish and. And I love the domestic arts. I like to do embroidery and cooking and. And I like to iron even, but, but I really, I. I love the out of doors things. And, uh, and I think that the sense of independence that I had by growing up on ranches in the summertime and having a. I bought my first car when I was 16. I saved money from jobs I did in the summer, and I went to work out at the construction company. I worked for, Peter Johnson, who was working for my father as a vice president of personnel, and he is the one who started telling me about eastern schools for women, which my father had wanted me to go to Stanford and took me there when I was only 14, so that I would take all the classes that I needed to get into Stanford, which I did. And then Peter thought it would be more broadening for me to go east. So I did, much to my father's chagrin. I went to Wellesley College and it was culture shock. I was not like anybody else there. I was very mature socially and, you know, in the management of money and my own affairs and and transportation. I used to take horses over to Oriana to get them trained. When I was 15 years old, I was driving a pickup and a horse trailer by myself with a horse, but they wouldn't even let me rent a car. When I got to Boston, and yet I was extremely immature intellectually. All those girls had tremendous backgrounds in in the knowledge of history of the world and several foreign languages and, and advanced mathematics and science. We just didn't have those courses here. We'd never heard of advanced placement courses or college. Getting college credit in high school like they do now. So I felt very much behind the group when I got there, but I still have some great friends that I met there. And what did you decide to study when you got. Well, I was best. I was best in English and foreign languages. I had a natural affinity for writing and I love to read always, but I felt the gap mostly in what has happened up to now. So I majored in history to find out what has happened, and I'm still reading history to find out what has happened. Well, we have that in common. Most certainly. Um, while you were on the East Coast, uh, did you have any opportunities To, like you said, build connections with others. What were some of those friendships like? Oh gosh, I am so grateful that the, the kind of people that I met at Wellesley were really, really top notch kind of people. I remember being invited in those days. It was not unusual for our parents to drink cocktails in the evening. And I remember I was only 18, but I was invited to the home of my friend Sue Coates in New York City on Riverside Drive, and they didn't think a thing about telling me which subway stop to get off of. I had never been on a subway before by myself. I had no idea where Riverside Drive was, but I didn't want to sound like an ignoramus, so. So I got on the subway and I followed their directions and they were perfect. And I got there. But I was really kind of scared, you know? And then I got there and they just treated me like a regular adult. And I had a cocktail with them and and they were just wonderful people. I remember particularly one of the first times I ever went to was in Providence, Rhode Island. Mimi Fish was a tiny, delicate little girl that unfortunately, I just read of her death this year, but, um, she. Her mother was not only a medical doctor, but a psychiatrist. Being from the logging camp, the ranch in Boise, Idaho, I had never met a woman who was even a medical doctor before in my life. At that time, women were not medical doctors. And so I was bowled over to think that this minuscule, brilliant Jewish woman was not only a medical doctor, but but a psychiatrist with many more years of study. And they were so kind and generous to me, and they, I'm sure, thought of me as an anomaly, which I was. Being from the Far West, and they. And they asked me a lot of questions about my home, which were quite. Was, you know, obviously very different from anything there. And, um, my experience growing up and so forth, they were just very gracious and hospitable and made me feel accepted and warm. And to be invited for a weekend in somebody's home was a special thing because I didn't know anybody back there. Nobody. It was almost 3000 miles from home, and it took me two and a half days to get there on the train. Wow. Yeah. Wow. And then Judy Duarte lived on what was called the main line, which meant the train line from Philadelphia. Uh, I forget which direction the main line is. I think it's north, but she lived at one of the suburbs of Philadelphia. that the stop on the main line. And I went to her house several times. She and her husband have come to the ranch many years since then, and Sue Coates came to the ranch and Sue and a friend from Oklahoma who now is an attorney. Well, she's retired now, but was an attorney in Rochester, New York, for years. Came and visited me at the ranch. So I've kept in touch with these women who were so outstanding, and they're still part of my life. And thank you for that answer. Um, when you graduated college, did you finish at at Wellesley or did you go somewhere else? No, I, I just, I really missed being with men. My whole life was really male centered living, you know, in the kind of businesses my father had. Having only brothers. Um, I started dating when I was very young, I was I, I still remember my first date was when I was only 11 years old, and this little boy named Tyrone invited me to go to a movie at the Ada theater, which is now the Egyptian. And it was when the orchid was. The organ was still playing there, and they're going to do a renovation project. I was just talking to Anita Kehati yesterday, and they're doing a renovation of the organ, the old organ, it's underneath the stage and they're going to bring it up. It's going to cost about a half $1 million to do this. So I'm going to help her with that. But, uh, it's, uh, it's still a memory that this darling little boy named Tyrone invited me to go to a movie, and I can't remember what movie it was, but I still remember that organ, uh, playing before the movie. His. We were so little. We were in the sixth grade. His mother picked me up at my house and dropped us off in the afternoon on a Saturday and then came back to get us when the movie was over. And that boy didn't go to high school with me. I have no idea what happened to him. Wow. But, uh, anyway. It's interesting, the things that we remember. I went on from, um, from Wellesley to. I did it quite a bit of dating at Wellesley, and I didn't remember that till I looked at my old diary last summer. I was doing some autobiographical stuff and, and, and I met a lot of men through mixers, but they were, they were men that were so different from the men I knew. It was interesting, but I missed not only men. I missed my freedom. Uh, I missed having my own car. I missed, um, movement when I wanted to go places. Uh, so I went to Berkeley. I, I had a, I had a, a applied to. Wellesley, Smith and Stanford and I had been admitted to all of them. But when I was a junior, I didn't get the perfect grades at Wellesley. It was so hard. I, I. Applied to four schools Stanford, Berkeley, uh, UW and one in Texas. And, and I, I did not get into Stanford because it's hard to transfer. And so I did get into Berkeley and it had more Nobel Prize winners. And it was an urban environment, which was also a shock to me. I'd never lived in an urban environment before. And so I went to Berkeley the last two years and majored in history, European history, and minored in romance languages. So I had gone to Spain this summer after my sophomore year and received a certificate in the Spanish language. The whole. All of the instruction was in Spanish, so I'm fluent in Spanish still. And then I began studying French at Berkeley, and I take French lessons from Madame Applequist every Saturday now. Um, but I got a later, later in life, I went back to school and I got a master's degree at BSU in history. That was just general history that was available. I took Greek history and methods and became very close to Pat Doctor Patricia Rada, who became a mentor of mine. And then I went to Georgetown and I got a master's in international studies there. And they're in a program for people who came. A lot of them worked for the federal government, and they were at late afternoon evening classes. And so I went there for a couple of years, and I did a thesis on El Salvador. Um, that was when the war was down there. So it was mainly on Latin American affairs. But when I came back, I was hoping to teach at BSU. That spot was full. So I didn't teach that. I ended up teaching a lot of, uh, courses that were broad survey courses of early America and late America. And then I got a PhD at the University of New Mexico in Albuquerque, and my specialty ended up coming right back to home. And I did a book on Boise that came out of my dissertation, which was, um. Suggested by Merl Wells, who was one of my teachers at BSU as in independent studies. And, uh, it was about, um, the research question was if, if the two main things. In early Boise were the development of the irrigation system and the development of the transportation system, which they were, and they both failed. Why did Boise become the center anyway? So that was fun. And the answer is, I thought in the in the wonderful topography here, the the fact that it's such a nice valley to live in climate wise and topography wise, and the fact that the people who came and settled here after the gold mining and during the timber and starting the start of agriculture here, uh, really were do it yourselfers. They, they, they created a community that they wanted to live in and they were independent people. They were in an isolated place. And if they wanted an opera, which they had in the 19th century, and history society, which they had in the 19th century, and a. Hospitals which they had in the 19th century, at the very get go of their community, they had to do it themselves. They had to raise the money. They had to have the expertise. They had to they had to do things themselves. So I loved the the story about the independent spirit here and do it up the entrepreneurs who made this a beautiful place to live. Even though the railroad bypassed them and didn't come here until 1925, and even though the irrigation thing just languished and and they finally finished certain parts of it throughout the early 20th century, the people just wanted to be here and they created a beautiful community. So it was a community that had a wonderful philanthropic history. I am familiar with your work. I've used it in some of my projects. And, and, um, we'll have a chance to circle back to how your professional work in history in that field has maybe influenced your philanthropic interests as well. Thank you for that answer. Um, one of the things that I learned when preparing the questionnaire for today was that you worked for a stent, a small stent, uh, for Senator Jordan, who was one of Idaho's, uh, congressional delegates. I was hoping you could tell us a little bit about how you got that position and what that was like. Um, Senator Jordan was on my father's board of my McGregor triangle company, and I knew him my whole life. Um, so it was a personal connection, I guess you'd say. But, you know, I think my father kind of pulled one on me because he, um. I told him that I wanted to go to New York and work for the United Nations. And I know that he wasn't scintillated by that idea. And he said, well, why don't you ask Senator Jordan to help you get that position and then tell him that you're available if if it doesn't come through? So that's how I got it. I became the research assistant for Senator Jordan, and I worked for him for two years. I lived in Georgetown and I had a lot of fun. What did you see in D.C. during that time that, you know, again, was maybe different than your upbringing here that, um, led you to, again, develop an interest in community service and volunteerism and giving back. Um. I. I, I found Washington, D.C. a very nice place to live because there were so many young people who were working for senators and, and Representatives. And so it was, it was pretty easy to, to have fun there. And there were lots of parties, but I don't know what really what led me to, um, after work engage in a philanthropic project of the receiving home for children. It was called, it was really a kiddie jail. And uh, when I first went there, I felt so bad that I was really glad that I wanted to become part of talking. It was mainly black boys. There were over 70 in this one concrete room milling around without furniture. These boys were turned there by their parents, by their social workers or by the police. They were awaiting trial. They weren't even convicted criminals. And it was the saddest thing. So I would just go once a week with a couple of friends of mine from Senator Jordan's office, and we would just talk to these boys. And just so they'd have some company. They didn't have anybody even visiting them, and they would choose certain boys that were on good behavior as sort of a reward for them just to be able to talk to people. So that was a real eye opener for me, that that such a place could even exist and how horrible it was. And I'm sure that was outlawed. And it's not possible for such a thing to exist today. But I'll tell you, it was a gruesome thing for those boys. They, they really they were fed and they had a bathroom and that was about it. Wow. Wow. Um, I know that over the course of the late 1960s, you had some time that you spent in Seattle working for Alaska Airlines and that ultimately you came back to Boise in the early 1970s. Um, and you met, uh, Harry Bettis, who became your husband. Um, I was hoping you could tell me what that transition was like coming back to Boise and coming back to this area. Well, I came back because I was divorced from my first husband. I had a child when in 1968, I married a man who was born in Argentina. He was half German and half Italian. And, uh, he, he was, uh, I met him in Washington, D.C., and, uh. It didn't work. Things didn't work out. So I came back here and, um, I moved in with my mother for a little while and paid her rent. My mother and And, um. Soon found an apartment in the North End. I worked for Pete Cenarrusa for $2 an hour as the, quote unquote expert on microfilm. I didn't know what microfilm was one day. And then I was in charge of microfilm the next day. So I had to really learn fast how to put everything on microfilm. That was that was the one of the earliest technologies of how to save things instead of files. So, so that's what I did. And I did that part half time and I went to back to BSU half time and I took 18 credits, half time of education courses because I decided I was going to raise my son in Idaho and I wanted to be able to, um. Live here and, and so I would become a teacher and the education courses were quite easy. So I took 18 credits. And then before I was through with that, this, this man appeared in my life who kept hounding me to go teach in Emmet. So I came here in the fall. I came here in August, started BSU, and before January this man appeared and wanted me to go teach French and Spanish at Emmet High School. And the first time he came, I said, oh, I could teach French one and Spanish one and Spanish two, but I. French two would be pretty hard for me to teach and I need to do my practice teaching, so I don't think so. Then he came back and he said, this woman is about to deliver a baby, and we can't find anyone in the state of Idaho to take this position. And so I said, okay, I will study to be able to teach French to. If. And I will find a place to live in Emmett. I, I know a place where I can rent in Emmett with my son. If you will give me credit for practice teaching and pay me because I can't afford to not give up a semester and then go back to school to get practice teaching. And he said right away that he would do it. So that's what I did. I moved over to Mern Little's, uh, maid's quarters in the old Andy little mansion in Emmett, Idaho. And I had known her all through my youth, and her daughters were my good friends. One of them was the same age. We had ridden horses all over Long Valley together and crippled a few, renting them too much. We'd had a lot of fun, and so JJ and I moved in Burns House in January. I taught those four classes and I had four minutes in between period four and period five to get in my car from the high school drive to the junior high and teach two classes of French one and Spanish one. And kids by that time were throwing erasers all over the board and destroying the classroom. I had the hardest time getting Carberry to monitor that classroom for only three minutes, because I could make it in seven minutes, but not four. And so that was that was interesting. So I taught at Emmett High and, and junior High. And the 28th of January was David Little's birthday. And that's Brad's father. And he was the youngest of the five children of Andy Little, who was the biggest sheep rancher in America in the 1920s. So, uh, I went to David Little's birthday party and his nephew Harry was there. I had met Harry before either of us had ever married. He was eight years older than I, and I had met him at Aggie Ale's wedding. Aggie ale was his cousin who was my age, and I met him at his grandfather Little's mansion at her wedding. And. And then, uh, you know, time had passed. He hadn't married and divorced. I had married and divorced. And I went to that birthday party, and he asked me if he could show me his ranch. And I said, well, I, I that would be on the weekend. I spend, I spend the weekends with my child. Oh, I meant for him to come too. He said So he came in a big red pickup that smelled kind of funny. And we all went out to his ranch and he cooked meal for us and took us riding on his hay wagon with his workhorses pulling it. And, uh, quipped that his mother had gone to Wellesley, and he thought it was pretty cool that he had a Wellesley girl driving his team. So that's how it started. And then we went to horse races and various things, and we were married that August. And once married, did you land in Boise again or. Oh, no. Oh no, I was I landed at Willow Creek where it's very muddy in the spring, very muddy in the winter, frozen in the winter and dry in the summer. And, uh. I cooked for his hired men. So I learned how to make bread. I baked pies constantly. I had dinner on breakfast on the table at 6:30 a.m.. Dinner was served at noon. A huge meal and supper was at 6:00 at night and was usually leftovers from dinner. And he told me to make lots of bread and lots of dessert because that's what the men liked. And it was cheap. So that's what I did. Interesting. Well, I want to bring us back kind of into Boise and into the work of Junior League and your role and involvement in that organization. Um, junior League of Boise is one of almost 300 chapters of the Junior League organization that was first started in 1901 in New York City by Mary Harriman. Um, Boise's league first selected or first expressed interest in joining and getting a charter in 1925, and they were successful. By 1928 they became the 96th league in the world. That was because of the party at Mrs. Cunningham's house. So I want to talk about that. Um, but I, I wanted to get, um, get you to kind of explain how you learned about junior league and what the process was like, uh, when you first joined and our records indicate you were a provisional in 1978. Yes. I was still married to Harry and I was living, um, at the ranch, and then I came over to live here for a year because, um, Catherine was born in 1976. Um, so, um, I was 35 years old before I joined the the Junior League. And you have to retire when you're 40. So I had a brief but interesting, wonderful career with the Junior League. I became the Vice President of all the projects during that year before my. I think when I was 39. And, um, I'll tell you, it's still really valuable to me. The training they gave us then the training was fabulous in the Junior League. I remember particularly them talking about something that you don't learn in college at all, at least the places I went to college. They never mentioned personal development or personal care or financial care or anything like that. And the Junior League really addressed the whole person of a woman. And the Junior League at that time in particular, was interested in the changing role of women. Some of the women at that time were working. The majority of them were not employed by other people outside their own home. So there was a there was not. A uniformity of perspective, even among members about this. But I thought that the training of the Junior League was stellar because it addressed the individual. And I remember this pie that was divided in three parts, three equal parts. May I say one of them was your work. Maybe your work was at home. Maybe your work was in philanthropy. Maybe your work was at the First National Bank or any other place downtown. anyway. A third of it was your work. In other words, that it's a woman has purpose to to get something done. And a third of it was our relationships. Well, this is the part that the Junior League and it's, uh. Had perceived was enveloping the psyche of most women. Heretofore, women were spending most of their time on other people than themselves. And so it was brilliant to me. And it still is that a third of it. Our lives are our relationships, i.e. our children, our husband, our friends. All of these are relationships. And a third of it, guess what was for you, for you, for your intellectual development, for your spiritual beliefs for your sleep and physical care and exercise. Take care of yourselves so that you have something in the well to pull out of, to give to others, to give to your work, to give to your relationships. That if you don't spend time with the third that is you, that you really can't be effective in the other two. And I just still find that very helpful in my life and refer to it still. Yeah. When you received that training, was that part of the onboarding as a provisional member? Yes. Okay. What were the other requirements of provisional members at the time that you joined? Oh, we had to we, we had a rigorous requirement of attendance at certain meetings and training. Yeah, we had, we had, uh, mentors whose homes we went to occasionally. Uh, I broke up a tete a tete at one of them. It was interesting. Anyway, I was there on time, and I guess I stayed too long or something. Somebody appeared. It was hilarious, but. No, uh, it was, uh. You know, the Junior League at that time was a great training ground for women. I don't, I don't think that it is anymore. And I think that the, the profile of the average member has changed entirely. So I think that was the heyday of the Junior League. The training programs that they had at that time were stellar. And the and the, the workforce they had in those women who were not employed outside the home to do the thing they called the Follies, which I was never a part of. But I'm sure you will get some great interviews about the kinds of energy about these big projects that the Junior League had to engage. The talents of these women were phenomenal and and absolutely impossible to do now. Yeah. What do you think is the biggest challenge to generating that kind of interest or that kind of motivation or energy with our current membership? Well, I don't think it's possible at all to, to compare that kind of energy that existed then and that kind of enthusiasm. And you're talking about some women who are now in their 80s who went to sororities and were rah rah and, and grew up in colleges where there was a spirit that's entirely different from now. And, and the country itself. Was different from the country. Now there's a there's a lot of disillusionment now. And I'm not saying that that one is bad or good. I'm saying that it cannot exist. That kind of spirit that that happened then, and the kind of energy and the kind of commitment that people gave for other than self, uh, is impractical. Now, these women have jobs and, and, and, uh, the purpose of the junior league has changed, I think. From the research I've done in the league records, looking at how certain projects evolved and how, uh, the leaders kind of made decisions about where to focus their time and their attention. Um, the thing that has stood out to me from that work is the breadth of projects that the league has touched over time. And you mentioned you were the VP or vice president. What were some of those big projects that you remember being involved with? Well. I remember we started the Discovery Center. You know, going back to the 20s, I lived in a home that still had the forsythia on Warm Springs Avenue in the in the 1920s when they first got started. They decided to plant forsythia all the way up Warm Springs Avenue and down the other side. And my home was one of the few that still had it there. And the people, the current owners have ripped it out. But but I mean, they've done everything from urban beautification to alcohol in schools was one of the projects that was beginning when I was there. They, we had members go talk to kids in junior high about alcoholism and what it causes and show videos and, and educate kids about alcohol. Some of them had drunken parents and some of them wanted to try it out and I. We. Lynn McCullum was my neighbor across the street. Started the Guardian ad litem program so that children in courts had reputable mentors, many of whom. Many of those children did not have parents that could advocate for them. There were fabulous programs from all spectrums. And I want to tell you an early memory I had from the Junior League. When I was in the second grade, second or third, but I think it was second, this woman came to Jefferson Grade School and she had a picture. I still remember that was a Diego Rivera picture. Wow. And she, uh, I had no art education whatsoever. I lived in a logging camp in a ranch. I mean, you know, I was a tomboy, but she showed us this picture of Diego Rivera and talked to us about such things as color and balance and brush strokes, and I never. The concept of any of those had never touched my mind before, but I still remember it. She brought in these different pictures and maybe there were some other ones. That's the only one I remember. But it opened a door in my mind that I still remember. And I'm, you know, 83 now. And I was seven then. So it was it's remarkable what one presentation can do to a child. Yeah. They had art in the schools then. And I remember, uh, that was active for many years too. Art in the schools. By the time I was vice president, it was alcohol out of the schools. But but we've always done a lot of educational, uh, projects and particularly for children, we've done beautification projects, we've done great performances to raise money and, and, um, learned how to raise money. And then, um, the YMCA at the YWCA at the time, uh, used some of our outstanding junior league women. I remember Julie Kilgrow and Julia Simplot were involved in women in management. That wasn't a Junior League project, but it belonged. It involved the same group of women that, uh, that women could be in management, that we didn't need to just be the typists for the men that told us what to write in shorthand and then type up on their typewriters. And, and I think it's very ironic that men have to type their own letters now, but, uh, you know, That was that was the 1970s. That that women could be in management. And then I became part of a group called International Women's Forum, and that was the whole purpose. It was started in New York City for the same sort of purposes that we could break this glass ceiling, that we could excel, that we could be as effective as any other human being. Um, you mentioned one of the projects or one of the opportunities was to learn how to fundraise and to learn how to ask for money. Um, throughout the league's history, both our chapter and more broadly, the development of cookbooks has often been one of those mechanisms for generating funds. Were you involved with the cookbook projects? I gave a lot of recipes, but I was not involved. I have bought the the green one at different thrift stores. I love it and I use it, but I'm the only person that I know of whose name didn't appear in it as a donor. And Ramona Hager still can't believe that. But I have 4 or 5 recipes in there and no credits and I don't care. It's fine. Well, you're on the record now noting that there's at least five that were yours. Um. Almond Beauvoir's was one of them. It was my cousin's recipe, and it was delicious. I love puddings. I will have to look that one up. I think I have that cookbook at home myself. Um, what else do you remember about how the league tried to fundraise in the 1970s or early 1980s? Was there something that differentiated it from other organizations at the time? We didn't, we didn't, um, we didn't we fundraised for the Junior league, but I think we didn't have a specific program teaching women how to do it, but I think the orientation. Let me tell you another one of the training things that I remembered that is helpful to me still. We talked about planning an event and the stages of not just an event, but it could be a happening or it it has to do with setting goals. And I've always been a goal driven person. I was just born that way, and I've always had goals my entire life, and I wish I could stop forming goals. I'm getting tired. But it stopped. It started with brainstorming. It broke down the stages of goal setting and what you have to do. And it's very helpful. First of all, you brainstorm things and it's helpful if you're in a group, if it's a group activity that everybody write down on a board, all of the different ideas of brainstorming that people come up with, then you. From those ideas that are all scattered, you form in a sentence. The goal. What is the goal here? The general goal. And from that goal, you hone it into a concept. And the concept has to say precisely what you're doing by a certain date that this is going to happen, that you are going to. Well, my latest goal is reading at least one of the uh, and I can't help but even in my own personal life, doing it the same way the Junior League taught me, uh, that I'm going to read at least one book by every Nobel Prize winner for literature. So, so I started this last August and I thought, okay, so first of all, I had to find out what they were who had won the Nobel Prize. And I found out that there were several years that they didn't give it at all. And then there were two years that they gave two of them, and then there was another year they gave it to a songwriter. And so there were in total, 122 books to read. And then I didn't want to limit myself to only one book. If this guy was or a woman was a great author, I might read more. But the goal still was to read one of 122 authors, and I found out I had already read 23 of them without knowing it, because everybody has read Hemingway and, and Scott Fitzgerald and, and a lot of the other famous ones of the 20th century. So then I started looking them up on Amazon and so forth of who these people were. And the first one was Sully Prudhomme. Sully Prudhomme, a French poet. So then you order his book and there's no English translation that I found. So I'm going very slowly through the first one because I'm translating French poems. But but then you go through the list and you say, oh, I have to do this by what time? And I thought, I don't need to push myself. I'm going to do it over three years. So you set a timeline. What is it you're going to do? What is the timeline that you're going to finish? Because if you don't have a finished date, you probably won't. So you have that's the concept phase. And then the, the actual plan is, is reading the books, you know, or, or, you know, whatever it is you're doing, that is your goal. And in, in the case of the junior League, it was usually like a great big party. They were going to have. So they would. Why were they going to do the party? What were they going to spend the money on? And then, you know, when were they going to have the party? Who would it involve? How many committees needed to do that? And then the committees would come together and it would happen on such and such a day. And the benefit would be for such and such a reason, and they would put on a big show that would be this A, B, C and D and F, you know. So it was a great it was a great way to say that you don't just pull this stuff out of your pocket, that it takes organization and planning, and you need to hone it from the general to the particular to make the thing come out and coordinate different people to be ready at the same time. And without that kind of planning, things get botched. So that was just it was very practical. And it was very, you know, it's common sense in a way. But I had always been kind of an individualist. I'd always kind of been a do it yourselfer, and I didn't really know how to coordinate things with other people until I found this, this wonderful tool of how you do it together to get to the same goal so that you're not. I've been in women's groups where they couldn't even decide how to spend $100, you know, and you sit there in this one club I had where I honestly quit because I couldn't, I didn't have the patience to listen to it. You know, that women often have had a hard time because they want to defer to each other, but they want their own way and they don't have a procedure to go forth with. So I think the Junior League training about planning was very, very helpful. I love that answer. And even in my tenure in the league, which is, you know, much later than yours, I have found pockets of those kinds of inspirational tools or tangible resources that have helped me in my volunteer work and also in my in my professional world. So thank you for the story and the anecdote there. Um, based on that original training you had about your life in thirds, right? With people and work and yourself. Um, I was hoping you could talk about who were some of the people that you met through junior league and the relationships you did build that have been super impactful in your life? I met the most wonderful women in my provisional class who have become my friends since then. Uh, Bev Herrod, uh, Betsy Terwilliger, pursley now, uh, Candy Weaver. We had the best, most wonderful provisional class of people that are still my friends. And my participation now in the league is sort of laughable. I'm doing easy things. I'm having lunch with junior leaguers at the Shore Lodge in the summertime once a month. I'm in the pearls group even though I don't knit. I needlepoint and embroider, so I go and needlepoint and embroider while they're knitting. And we put together some years presents to be bought at the Christmas raffle for the Christmas luncheon. But I've enjoyed people like Alice Hennessey and Lindy. Hi. Um, I've known Alice for years. Uh. And I just enjoy it so much. And then, um. I meet with them for bridge here when I'm here in Boise for four months in the winter time. In fact, next day after tomorrow, I'm going to play bridge with the Junior League group. And it's just it's an association of very interesting involved women. Mhm. So I'm continuing those relationships. Julie Killgrove and I have been friends. She was president of the Junior League when I first joined. And Jan Jan Birgersson became a very good friend of mine. She was another president in my early years there, and I have helped her with her horse. When we went to some riding a riding group over in Oregon, I would go with my horse trailer and truck and pick up her horse, and we'd go off riding together and we'd. I've had many wonderful friendships of people that I met in the Junior League. Excellent. So we've kind of reached the point in the interview where we're going to start to wrap things up a little bit. Um, so these questions are maybe a little bit more reflective than remembering what maybe had happened or not. Um, but throughout your life, you have clearly been involved in a number of organizations that have done philanthropic works and you've been supportive of the arts and the humanities and history and all of the work that you've done in your ranching career. I was hoping that you could kind of think broadly across all of that work and speak to the project or the initiative that you're most proud of. Raising for good kids. Is my best work, and I'm a very active and grandmother now. And I did, um, use my investment expertise that I honed after a divorce in 1981 with Harry. Um. To invest in, um, some 520 nines that are paying for their grandchildren's education. So I'm most proud of being able to do that, I guess. But I love my forestry work at the ranch because I have, unlike most ranchers, spent quite a bit of time in calling the least desirable species and in getting rid of dead dying and diseased trees and creating an eco culture that is truly beautiful makes the land a more productive for cattle and makes it more beautiful for other uses in the future. And, uh, so I, um, I have done what the US Forest Service has not done. I have not let it pile up and burn. And so I have endured two horrible forest fires in the last four years, and neither of which came across my fence, but both of which caused a lot of Forest Service workers to go through my ranch to get to the burn. So I'm very proud of my forestry work on the ranch and the shape of my ranch. And, and I'm very proud of, of, um, of the opera that just happened. Um, I produced a, a underwrote an opera Thais at Opera Idaho, and I've been a board member, a singer and an advisory board member and a worker at Opera Idaho for 30 some years. And I Kind of. Directed quite a bit of my funds toward the arts here in Idaho. Because I know, I know the redneck culture here very well. Having the background that I do as a tomboy. But, um, I know that many people in Idaho are more apt to support football than they are the arts, and support a lot of other endeavors before they support the arts. So I've, I love the arts. I love, I love, um, opera, especially because it's, it's theater, it's symphony, it's voices. And we did a great collaboration with Ballet Idaho for this last opera. So, uh, I, I think it's opera's colorful and interesting and, and I've gone to the Metropolitan Opera quite a bit too. And they have it live in HD here, but I've been in New York too. So I've tried to support the arts and, and I don't know really how much difference I've made in that, but but I know that this opera was very well received and would not have happened if I hadn't underwritten it and suggested it. And the response was very positive. So I don't know that there's one thing. But my priorities have to be family first, and then and then my community. And, um, and, um, I'm very, I'm very grateful that I was born in Idaho. It's a very new part of the world for Europeans. We're only we're less than 200 years old here. We're very young And, uh, I think Boyce is a beautiful community. And, uh, it's changing with the influx of population, but it still has a lot of the essence that I've enjoyed throughout my life. Yeah. Uh. Some of what you provided in that answer prompts me to ask, uh, for you to reflect kind of on the, the changing world more broadly, right? We're kind of in an environment right now where we're seeing a lot of changes happening to our systems of government, where we see shifting priorities at the federal and national level, um, budget cuts, coming to organizations who advocate for the arts and the humanities. Um, if this trend continues or if it doesn't, what do you think the role of private philanthropy, private philanthropy is going to be in Idaho moving forward? I think private in my book, Boise, Idaho, 1882 to 1910, Prosperity and isolation. The central premise is that people in this isolated place have continuously reached their pockets to make things happen. If you look at national statistics, individual philanthropy is more significant than corporate philanthropy. You pay for corporate philanthropy. When you go to Albertsons, they want you to donate to their charity so they can take the write off for your money. I mean, corporate philanthropy has a long ways to go. And I, I think it's an untapped Relatively untapped resource that I'm going to try to get some of my favorite philanthropies to work on better. And I did that when I was a board member of the Boise Art Gallery. We had some wonderful receptions for new corporations then that don't even exist here now. But I think that, uh, I think that it is not going to change as far as the role of private philanthropy. I think that private philanthropy is extremely important. And there's a lot of people who think that government should pay for everything. And, and government has paid for a lot of things. And that's the trend now is to go the other way. But private philanthropy will continue. Corporate philanthropy, I hope, will increase. And government philanthropy is, you know, very touchy, wicked because. Government is all of us and we all have such diverse opinions and no. No time in my life or very few times in in the course of our Republic have things been more diabolically, diametrically opposed as they are now. The. The lack of civility in the body politic is very high and. And this too will pass. We, I, I think that's why history is a more interesting topic than, than political science, because the historian analyzes both sides and makes generalizations and and analytical predictions and reactions. Whereas the politician gets out in marches and and signs petitions and and bitches and moans. And I think that I'm sick of hearing the bitching and moaning from my friends on both sides. And I have friends who are liberals and friends who are conservatives, and I'm neither one. But I. Think it's a waste of breath and I do not watch television at all. I do not pay for a television service at this point. At either my ranch or this home. I'm going to have to probably bite the bullet and try to figure out some satellite connection for the ranch, because I'm there most of the year. But I don't want to hear people interrupting each other. I don't want to hear people's opinions on everything. It's repetitive. It's boring. It's usually ill said. I do believe in the basic goodness of the premise of our government here in the United States. I think it was the most brilliant idea yet, and it has played out, for better or worse, over 250 years now. And I this is not going to be the end of it. This is the current administration will be gone in a couple of years, and another one will come. And it is up to us as voters and citizens to advocate for good leadership. I would like very much for the age limit on leadership to be set. I would like for members of the Supreme Court, the executive branch and the legislative branch to be under 75 years old and maybe 70. I am tired of hearing old ideas and tired of the hubris that has been displayed. By elder statesmen who can't give up. I would like very much for that to be enacted. I do believe that we will get by this, just as we've gotten by a lot of other things. I, I hope and pray that the Cuban people can be free people again. I think Cuba's I've visited 96 countries in the world so far, and the worst one almost is Cuba. The people are very oppressed there. I've spoken with several of them in Spanish. Apart from the rest of the crowd where we weren't heard and they all want to come here. They all have relatives here. They all envy our freedom of movement and speech. So I hope that something will come out of that. I I'm glad that that drug kings are being stopped. But I wonder, you know, how I don't want Iran to have a bomb, but I don't know that what we're doing now is going to solve that. So I just I'm not a pundit on current affairs and I certainly don't I'm not a whiner and I don't like to listen to whining. I would like to see, you know, this will play out. I, I basically do have faith. I have faith in our government. I have faith in God. I have faith in in the ideals of this country. I have hope also for all of those. And I have love for my family and friends. So I think, you know, the, the old, uh, you know, the biblical kind of idea of faith, hope and love, I think is real. Uh, the human beings have to have that to be able to endure. And the people I know who have died, who had none of those were very unhappy people. Thank you for that answer. Um, I've only have a couple more questions, but I think this is a good one to kind of end on. Uh, in December of 2025, the Junior League awarded you the Maryland Schuler Lifetime Achievement Award. This award recognizes a woman who has dedicated years to championing a cause in our community, and this woman should be acknowledged As a knowledgeable and responsible advocate, leader and mentor who is committed to the betterment and enrichment of individual lives in the community and as a whole, while also promoting a positive environment. Marilyn Schuller was a member of Junior League and a very influential advocate for human rights here in Idaho. She was a friend of mine. I knew her well. She was in my Spanish class. Interesting. Uh, I was hoping that you could reflect on what it was like for you to receive that award. It was very humbling, very humbling and surprising. Um, they, they did it in a different format than usual. They used to describe the woman. We'd all be sitting there guessing who she was, and we could pretty much tell by the time they didn't do it that way this year. They, um, they just called me up there and I was very, very surprised and, and really overwhelmed. Uh. I don't like to cry publicly, but I think I did and it was, it was, um, it was very meaningful to me to be recognized and I, I would, I could only in my wildest dreams hope that the work that I've done in philanthropy has made a significant difference for Boise. I have worked at philanthropy for 30 years as far as organizing what I would give monetarily and how I would give it. And I've tried to be very businesslike about about my approach to it instead of haphazard. And I think that comes partly from the skills that I learned at the Junior League about organizing things ahead of time and being, you know, that you are in charge of your own life. You you are the CEO of your life. What are what are you trying to get done and how are you going to divide that pie so that your relationships and your work? And part of my work is philanthropy. Part of it is investing money to be able to give and to be able to, to support my grandchildren's education and so forth. Part of it is, is the giving away of that money. And I think that that it needs to be in a logical kind of format and serve the purposes that I see are most needful for this community. So it has been a pleasure to be able to participate here, and I'm grateful I was born here. I'm grateful I still live here. And I love Idaho. Excellent. Uh, well, Carol, was there anything else that you wanted to talk about or share with us today that we didn't have a chance to talk about yet? I can't think of it. Thank you. Well, with that, we will conclude our our oral history and I will stop the recorder.

lines.line: # Introduction and Purpose of Interview

> The interviewer introduces herself and the interviewee, Carol McGregor, and sets the stage for an oral history interview focused on Carol's life, her impact in Idaho, and her involvement with the Junior League of Boise. The purpose and context of the interview are explained, including its connection to the Idaho State Historical Society and the America 250 in Idaho commemoration. The interviewer outlines the structure and goals of the conversation, seeking insights into Carol's motivations, experiences, and the changing opportunities for women.

metadata.title: Introduction and Purpose of Interview
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer introduces herself and the interviewee, Carol McGregor, and sets the stage for an oral history interview focused on Carol's life, her impact in Idaho, and her involvement with the Junior League of Boise. The purpose and context of the interview are explained, including its connection to the Idaho State Historical Society and the America 250 in Idaho commemoration. The interviewer outlines the structure and goals of the conversation, seeking insights into Carol's motivations, experiences, and the changing opportunities for women.

lines.line: # Early Life and Family Background

> Carol describes her birth and family history, including her grandfather's arrival in Idaho as a logger for the Weyerhaeuser Company and the family's connection to the lumber industry. She shares memories of growing up on ranches, her early experiences with horses, and the unique environment of logging camps. Carol recounts the positive atmosphere of the logging community and the influence of hardworking men, as well as her family's move to Boise following her grandmother's death.

metadata.title: Early Life and Family Background
metadata.synopsis: Carol describes her birth and family history, including her grandfather's arrival in Idaho as a logger for the Weyerhaeuser Company and the family's connection to the lumber industry. She shares memories of growing up on ranches, her early experiences with horses, and the unique environment of logging camps. Carol recounts the positive atmosphere of the logging community and the influence of hardworking men, as well as her family's move to Boise following her grandmother's death.

lines.line: # Schooling and Childhood in Boise

> Carol discusses her schooling in Boise, recalling her teachers, the development of local schools, and her progression through grade school, junior high, and high school. She describes her father's entrepreneurial ventures, including road construction and ranching, and the family's acquisition of several ranches. Carol shares vivid memories of ranch life, her love for horses, and the friendships formed with other ranch families, highlighting the joys and challenges of growing up as a tomboy in rural Idaho.

metadata.title: Schooling and Childhood in Boise
metadata.synopsis: Carol discusses her schooling in Boise, recalling her teachers, the development of local schools, and her progression through grade school, junior high, and high school. She describes her father's entrepreneurial ventures, including road construction and ranching, and the family's acquisition of several ranches. Carol shares vivid memories of ranch life, her love for horses, and the friendships formed with other ranch families, highlighting the joys and challenges of growing up as a tomboy in rural Idaho.

lines.line: # Values and Independence from Upbringing

> Carol reflects on the values instilled in her by her upbringing, emphasizing her independence, responsibility, and eagerness to please her parents. She describes the trust placed in her at a young age, her maturity, and the differences between her childhood and modern parenting practices. Carol discusses her early work experiences, her drive to save money, and her exposure to college options, ultimately leading to her decision to attend Wellesley College.

metadata.title: Values and Independence from Upbringing
metadata.synopsis: Carol reflects on the values instilled in her by her upbringing, emphasizing her independence, responsibility, and eagerness to please her parents. She describes the trust placed in her at a young age, her maturity, and the differences between her childhood and modern parenting practices. Carol discusses her early work experiences, her drive to save money, and her exposure to college options, ultimately leading to her decision to attend Wellesley College.

lines.line: # College Years and Cultural Adjustment

> Carol explains her academic interests, choosing to major in history to fill gaps in her knowledge and continuing to read history throughout her life. She recounts the cultural shock of attending Wellesley, the advanced backgrounds of her peers, and her feelings of intellectual inadequacy. Carol shares stories of friendships formed with classmates from diverse backgrounds, including memorable visits to their homes and the impact of meeting accomplished women, which broadened her perspective.

metadata.title: College Years and Cultural Adjustment
metadata.synopsis: Carol explains her academic interests, choosing to major in history to fill gaps in her knowledge and continuing to read history throughout her life. She recounts the cultural shock of attending Wellesley, the advanced backgrounds of her peers, and her feelings of intellectual inadequacy. Carol shares stories of friendships formed with classmates from diverse backgrounds, including memorable visits to their homes and the impact of meeting accomplished women, which broadened her perspective.

lines.line: # Post-College Education and Academic Pursuits

> Carol details her academic journey after Wellesley, including transferring to Berkeley, studying European history and romance languages, and becoming fluent in Spanish. She describes her later pursuit of a master's degree at BSU, a master's in international studies at Georgetown, and a PhD at the University of New Mexico. Carol's dissertation focused on Boise's development, exploring the city's independent spirit and philanthropic history, and she credits her teachers and mentors for guiding her research.

Carol's dissertation focused on Boise's development, exploring the city's independent spirit and philanthropic history, and she credits her teachers and mentors for guiding her research.

lines.line: # Early Professional Life and Work in Washington, D.C.

> Carol discusses her early professional experiences, including her personal connection to Senator Jordan and her role as his research assistant in Washington, D.C. She describes the vibrant community of young professionals in the city and her involvement in a philanthropic project at a children's receiving home. Carol recounts the eye-opening experience of interacting with boys awaiting trial in harsh conditions, which deepened her awareness of social issues and the need for community engagement.

metadata.title: Early Professional Life and Work in Washington, D.C.

lines.line: # Return to Idaho, Teaching, and Family Life

> Carol describes her return to Idaho following her divorce, her decision to raise her son in the state, and her work for Pete Cenarrusa. She explains her transition to teaching French and Spanish in Emmett, the challenges of managing multiple classes, and her living arrangements. Carol shares the story of meeting her future husband, Harry Bettis, their courtship, and her experiences living on the ranch, including cooking for hired men and adapting to rural life.

metadata.title: Return to Idaho, Teaching, and Family Life

She explains her transition to teaching French and Spanish in Emmett, the challenges of managing multiple classes, and her living arrangements. Carol shares the story of meeting her future husband, Harry Bettis, their courtship, and her experiences living on the ranch, including cooking for hired men and adapting to rural life.

lines.line: # Introduction to Junior League of Boise

> The conversation shifts to Carol's involvement with the Junior League of Boise, its history, and her initial experiences as a provisional member in 1978. Carol explains the circumstances of her joining, the age requirements, and her brief but meaningful tenure. She highlights the value of Junior League training, which addressed the whole person and emphasized the changing role of women, personal development, and the importance of balancing work, relationships, and self-care.

lines.line: # Junior League Training and Changing Role of Women

> Carol elaborates on the training she received in Junior League, including the concept of dividing life into thirds—work, relationships, and self-care. She discusses the organization's focus on personal development, the evolving role of women, and the differences in perspectives among members. Carol praises the training programs for their practical approach to women's lives and reflects on how these lessons continue to influence her.

metadata.title: Junior League Training and Changing Role of Women

lines.line: # Junior League Projects and Community Impact

> Carol discusses the challenges of generating enthusiasm and commitment among current Junior League members compared to earlier generations. She recalls the breadth of projects undertaken by the league, including the Discovery Center, urban beautification, educational programs, and advocacy for children. Carol shares a personal memory of art education in schools and highlights the league's impact on community development, women's leadership, and breaking the glass ceiling.

metadata.title: Junior League Projects and Community Impact
metadata.synopsis: Carol discusses the challenges of generating enthusiasm and commitment among current Junior League members compared to earlier generations. She recalls the breadth of projects undertaken by the league, including the Discovery Center, urban beautification, educational programs, and advocacy for children. Carol shares a personal memory of art education in schools and highlights the league's impact on community development, women's leadership, and breaking the glass ceiling.

lines.line: # Fundraising and Organizational Skills from Junior League

> Carol discusses the league's fundraising efforts, including cookbook projects and her contributions of recipes. She explains the practical organizational skills she learned, such as goal setting, event planning, and coordinating group activities. Carol illustrates how these skills have influenced her personal life, including her approach to reading Nobel Prize-winning authors, and emphasizes the importance of structured planning for successful outcomes.

lines.line: # Relationships and Friendships through Junior League

> Carol reflects on the lasting friendships and relationships she built through Junior League, naming several women who remain close friends. She describes her current involvement in league activities, such as lunch gatherings, needlework groups, and bridge games. Carol highlights the camaraderie and support among members, the enjoyment of shared interests, and the positive impact of these relationships on her life.

metadata.title: Relationships and Friendships through Junior League

Carol highlights the camaraderie and support among members, the enjoyment of shared interests, and the positive impact of these relationships on her life.

lines.line: # Philanthropic Work and Personal Achievements

> Carol considers her broader philanthropic work and personal achievements, expressing pride in raising her children and grandchildren, supporting their education, and her forestry work on the ranch. She describes her contributions to the arts, particularly Opera Idaho, and her efforts to support cultural initiatives in a region where such endeavors are often overlooked. Carol emphasizes the importance of family, community, and her gratitude for being born and living in Idaho.

metadata.title: Philanthropic Work and Personal Achievements

She describes her contributions to the arts, particularly Opera Idaho, and her efforts to support cultural initiatives in a region where such endeavors are often overlooked. Carol emphasizes the importance of family, community, and her gratitude for being born and living in Idaho.

lines.line: # Reflections on Philanthropy and Idaho's Future

> Carol offers her perspective on the evolving role of private philanthropy in Idaho, noting the significance of individual giving compared to corporate and government support. She discusses the challenges facing government funding for the arts and humanities, the importance of civic engagement, and her faith in the ideals of American democracy. Carol reflects on societal changes, the need for younger leadership, and her hope for continued progress and civility in public life.

metadata.title: Reflections on Philanthropy and Idaho's Future

lines.line: # Receiving the Marilyn Schuler Lifetime Achievement Award

> Carol shares her feelings about receiving the Marilyn Schuler Lifetime Achievement Award, describing the experience as humbling and surprising. She reflects on the significance of the recognition, her approach to philanthropy, and the influence of Junior League training on her organizational skills. Carol expresses gratitude for her ability to contribute to the community and her love for Idaho, concluding the interview with appreciation for the opportunity to share her story.
